titleStmt.title: The Chargé in Finland ( (Gullion) ) to the Secretary of State

div.head: The Chargé in Finland ( Gullion ) to the Secretary of State
div.p: 503. Stockholm please transmit code text this message to Department as my 503 Niact,Night action. US Urgent. Not otherwise necessarily Niact for Stockholm.

In view of inability to get anything but evasive answers on Ribbentrop’s presenceThe Chargé had reported in telegram 502, that day, that German Foreign Minister von Ribbentrop arrived in Helsinki on June 23. The actual arrival date was later reported as June 22; see telegram 515, June 27, from Helsinki, p. 605. I called on highest authority who confirmed his visit and said that he came on his own initiative without Finnish invitation. As to Finland’s course of action he claims that this is undecided. Project described in my secret 491, June 22,Not printed; it informed the Department that a proposed new Finnish Government would undertake to end immediately Finland’s co-belligerency with Germany, and that soundings to this effect had already been made with the Russians in Stockholm (740.00119EW1939/2695). said not to have materialized because soundings resulted in terms amounting to capitulation.The response of the Soviet Government to the eleventh-hour Finnish overture was that Finland must “capitulate”. Some details of these final, unsuccessful Finnish-Soviet exchanges can be found in despatch 3630, July 1, 1944, from Stockholm, not printed. (740.0019EW/7–144) My impression is that within a very short time Finland will reaffirm its solidarity with Germany. I did not fail to indicate to Foreign Minister impression this would cause in United States. I explained to him, however, that I was speaking without instructions.
